questions.question:
Choose the word from the options given below that is most nearly opposite in meaning to the given word:

Polemical

A. imitative  B. conciliatory  C. truthful  D. ideological

understand.plain: The question asks for the word that is most nearly opposite in meaning to 'polemical'. Polemical means strongly argumentative, controversial, or relating to aggressive debate and dispute. Its antonym must convey harmony, agreement, and a willingness to settle disputes peacefully. 'Conciliatory' fits perfectly since it means intended to make peace or reduce hostility.

keywords.explain: Relating to or involving strong, often controversial argument or dispute; aggressively critical or contentious in nature.
keywords.example: A polemical article attacks an opponent's position forcefully and provocatively.

keywords.explain: Intended to placate, make peace, or bring about goodwill; tending to reduce conflict rather than inflame it.
keywords.example: The negotiator took a conciliatory tone to ease tensions between the two parties.

keywords.explain: A word that is opposite in meaning to another word.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed for a vocabulary antonym question.

given.aim: Find the word most nearly opposite in meaning to 'Polemical'

terms.meaning: Strongly argumentative, controversial, combative in debate
terms.example: A polemical speech that sparked heated disagreement
terms.connects: Core word whose antonym is being sought

terms.example: An imitative style that mimics the original
terms.connects: Unrelated to the idea of debate or harmony; not an antonym

terms.example: A conciliatory gesture to end the argument
terms.connects: Directly opposes the combative nature of polemical

terms.meaning: Relating to a system of ideas or beliefs
terms.example: An ideological stance based on political belief
terms.connects: Can accompany polemical writing but is not its opposite

given.plan: Understand the core meaning of 'polemical' as dispute-inciting and confrontational, then identify which option carries the opposite sense of peace-making and harmony.

steps.title: Define the given word
steps.apply: 'Polemical' comes from the Greek 'polemos' (war/battle). It means strongly controversial, disputatious, or relating to aggressive argumentation and criticism.
steps.note: Words with roots in conflict or war signal a combative meaning.

steps.title: Eliminate weak options
steps.apply: 'Imitative' means copying — unrelated to the spectrum of argument or peace. 'Truthful' means honest — also unrelated to the combative dimension. 'Ideological' refers to belief systems — it can co-exist with polemical writing.
steps.note: Antonym search focuses on the core semantic dimension: conflict vs. harmony.

steps.title: Confirm the best antonym
steps.apply: 'Conciliatory' means intending to placate or make peace — the direct semantic opposite of the hostile, argumentative nature of 'polemical'. A polemical speech divides; a conciliatory response heals.
steps.note: The antonym must oppose the central meaning, not just peripheral associations.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas are needed; this is a vocabulary antonym question solved by semantic analysis.

questions.question:
Choose the word from the options given below that is most nearly opposite in meaning to the given word:

Deference

A. aversion  B. resignation  C. suspicion  D. contempt

understand.plain: 'Deference' means respectful submission or yielding to the judgment, authority, or opinion of another — it is a mark of high regard and respect. Its antonym must convey a lack of respect, even active disrespect. 'Contempt' is the feeling that someone or something is worthless or beneath consideration, making it the direct opposite.

keywords.explain: Humble submission and respect shown towards another person's authority, experience, or superior knowledge.
keywords.example: The junior officer showed deference to the general by following all orders without question.

keywords.explain: The feeling that someone or something is beneath consideration, worthless, or deserving scorn; the opposite of respect.
keywords.example: She could not hide her contempt for the dishonest official.

keywords.explain: Words can sit at opposite ends of a scale; respect-related words like deference and contempt occupy opposite poles on the scale of regard for others.
keywords.example: Admiration and disdain are on opposite ends of the esteem scale.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed for a vocabulary antonym question.

given.aim: Find the word most nearly opposite in meaning to 'Deference'

terms.meaning: Respectful submission or acknowledgement of another's authority
terms.example: In deference to his elders, he let them speak first
terms.connects: Core word — sits on the 'high respect' end of the regard scale

terms.meaning: A strong dislike or disinclination toward something
terms.connects: Relates to dislike but not specifically to disrespect for authority

terms.meaning: Acceptance of something undesirable but unavoidable
terms.example: He accepted his fate with resignation
terms.connects: Closer to passive acceptance; does not oppose the respect dimension

terms.meaning: Scorn; the feeling that someone is unworthy of consideration
terms.example: He looked at the corrupt official with contempt
terms.connects: Sits at the directly opposite end from deference on the respect scale

given.plan: Identify that deference lives on the high-respect end of an attitude scale, then pick the option that occupies the opposite (lowest-regard, most disrespectful) end of that same scale.

steps.title: Define the given word precisely
steps.apply: 'Deference' = respectful submission to another's authority or judgment. Key dimension: high positive regard / respect for someone.
steps.note: The word derives from Latin 'deferre' — to carry down, to yield.

steps.title: Check each option against the respect dimension
steps.apply: Aversion = dislike (wrong dimension — relates to preference, not respect). Resignation = passive acceptance (wrong dimension — relates to willingness, not regard). Suspicion = distrust (wrong dimension — relates to trust, not respect). Contempt = scorn / treating as worthless (correct dimension — directly negates respect).
steps.note: Only contempt is on the same axis (respect/regard) but at the opposite pole.

steps.title: Confirm D as the antonym
steps.apply: Deference involves elevating the other person in one's esteem. Contempt involves viewing the other person as beneath one's regard. These are polar opposites on the esteem axis.
steps.note: Contempt is sometimes defined as 'the opposite of respect' in lexical studies.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; the question is solved by lexical semantic analysis along the respect/esteem axis.

questions.question:
In 2022, June Huh was awarded the Fields medal, which is the highest prize in Mathematics. When he was younger, he was also a poet. He did not win any medals in the International Mathematics Olympiads. He dropped out of college.

Based only on the above information, which one of the following statements can be logically inferred with certainty?

A. Every Fields medalist has won a medal in an International Mathematics Olympiad.
B. Every chemist who dropped out of college has won the Fields medal.
C. All Fields medalists are part-time poets.
D. Some Fields medalists have dropped out of college.
questions.answer: D. Some Fields medalists have dropped out of college.
questions.to_find: which statement can be logically inferred with certainty from the passage

understand.plain: This is a logical inference question based on a short passage about June Huh. We must find a conclusion that is necessarily true given only the stated facts. The passage tells us one specific Fields medalist (June Huh) dropped out of college, which is sufficient to guarantee the truth of the 'some' statement in option D. Options A, B, and C either contradict the passage or go far beyond what the passage tells us.

keywords.explain: A conclusion that must be true whenever the given information is true — no additional assumptions allowed.
keywords.example: From 'John is a doctor', we can certainly infer 'Some doctors exist'.

keywords.explain: A statement of the form 'some X are Y' is true if at least one X is also Y.
keywords.example: From 'June Huh is both a Fields medalist and a college dropout', we can validly infer 'Some Fields medalists dropped out of college'.

keywords.explain: Incorrectly concluding a universal ('all') statement from a single instance ('one') — a common trap in inference questions.
keywords.example: Saying 'All crows are black' from seeing one black crow is a generalisation fallacy.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a verbal logical inference question.

given.aim: Identify the statement that is certainly true based only on the passage about June Huh

terms.meaning: June Huh received the Fields medal in 2022, the highest prize in Mathematics
terms.example: He is the first Korean-American to win the Fields medal
terms.connects: Establishes he is a member of the set 'Fields medalists'

terms.term: June Huh — dropped out of college
terms.meaning: The passage explicitly states he dropped out of college
terms.example: This fact combined with his Fields medal membership creates the existential truth
terms.connects: One Fields medalist dropped out => 'some Fields medalists dropped out' is true

terms.meaning: He did not win any medals in the International Mathematics Olympiads
terms.example: This directly refutes option A which claims every Fields medalist won an IMO medal
terms.connects: Contradicts option A, making it necessarily false

terms.meaning: Only information given in the passage may be used; no outside knowledge is allowed
terms.example: We cannot assume all Fields medalists are poets just because one was a poet
terms.connects: Rules out options B and C as unwarranted generalisations

given.plan: Test each option against the passage: check which are directly contradicted, which make unwarranted generalisations, and which are necessarily true from the given facts alone.

steps.title: Evaluate option A
steps.formula_raw: All A have property B = forall x (A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: Option A claims: Every Fields medalist won an IMO medal. But the passage says June Huh (a Fields medalist) did NOT win any IMO medal. This directly contradicts A. So A is false.
steps.note: A single counterexample is sufficient to disprove a universal statement.

steps.title: Evaluate option B
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false when A is true and B is false
steps.apply: Option B claims: Every chemist who dropped out of college has won the Fields medal. The passage mentions nothing about chemists at all. This is a wild generalisation far beyond the given information. B cannot be inferred with certainty.
steps.note: The passage is about June Huh specifically, not about a category of chemists.

steps.title: Evaluate option C
steps.formula_raw: All A have property B = forall x (A(x) -> B(x))
steps.apply: Option C claims: All Fields medalists are part-time poets. The passage only says June Huh was a poet in his youth. We cannot infer all other Fields medalists are also poets. C is an unwarranted generalisation.
steps.note: From 'one Fields medalist was a poet', we cannot conclude 'all are poets'.

steps.title: Evaluate option D
steps.formula_raw: Some A has property B = exists x (A(x) and B(x))
steps.apply: Option D claims: Some Fields medalists have dropped out of college. The passage tells us June Huh is a Fields medalist AND he dropped out of college. Therefore at least one Fields medalist dropped out, making 'some Fields medalists dropped out' certainly and necessarily true.
steps.note: One confirmed instance is all that is needed to validate an existential ('some') statement.

solution.result: D. Some Fields medalists have dropped out of college.

questions.formula_note: Logical inference rules (universal and existential quantifier translations) are used to test each option against the passage facts.

questions.question:
The World Bank has declared that it does not plan to offer new financing to Sri Lanka, which is battling its worst economic crisis in decades, until the country has an adequate macroeconomic policy framework in place. In a statement, the World Bank said Sri Lanka needed to adopt structural reforms to tackle the root causes of its crisis. The bank has starved it of foreign exchange and led to shortages of food, fuel, and medicines. The bank is considering existing loans to help alleviate shortages of essential items such as medicine, cooking gas, fertiliser, meals for children, and cash for vulnerable households.

Based only on the above information, which one of the following statements can be inferred with certainty?

A. According to the World Bank, the root cause of Sri Lanka's crisis is that it does not have enough foreign exchange.
B. The World Bank has stated that it will advise the Sri Lankan government on how to tackle the root causes of its economic crisis.
C. According to the World Bank, Sri Lanka does not yet have an adequate macroeconomic policy framework.
D. The World Bank will provide Sri Lanka with additional funds for essentials such as food, fuel, and medicines.
questions.answer: C. According to the World Bank, Sri Lanka does not yet have an adequate macroeconomic policy framework.
questions.to_find: which statement can be logically inferred with certainty from the passage

understand.plain: This passage reading question requires identifying the single statement that is directly and necessarily supported by the passage. The World Bank says it will not offer new financing until Sri Lanka has an adequate macroeconomic policy framework — this directly implies the Bank believes such a framework is currently absent. Options A, B, and D either misstate specific details, introduce unsupported conclusions, or conflict with the passage's wording.

keywords.explain: Drawing a conclusion that must be true given the stated facts — no inference beyond what the passage explicitly supports.
keywords.example: If a passage says a bank will lend 'only after condition X is met', we can infer the bank currently believes condition X is not yet met.

keywords.explain: When someone says 'I will do Y only when X happens', this implies that currently X has not happened.
keywords.example: 'Financing will resume when an adequate framework is in place' implies no adequate framework exists yet.

keywords.explain: A set of policies governing how a country manages its economy including fiscal policy, monetary policy, and structural reforms.
keywords.example: Sri Lanka was asked to implement an IMF-supported macroeconomic framework to access new loans.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a verbal inference question about a factual passage.

given.aim: Identify which statement is certainly inferable from the passage about World Bank and Sri Lanka

terms.meaning: No new financing until Sri Lanka has an adequate macroeconomic policy framework
terms.example: The conditional 'until X' implies X is not yet present
terms.connects: Directly supports option C — the framework is currently inadequate according to the Bank

terms.meaning: The passage says the Bank called for structural reforms to tackle root causes, but does not specify what those causes are
terms.example: The Bank mentions foreign-exchange shortages as effects, not necessarily as the named root cause
terms.connects: Rules out option A which claims the Bank specifically identified foreign-exchange shortage as the root cause

terms.meaning: The passage says the Bank declared it will not finance, not that it will advise the government
terms.example: Saying 'we won't lend until you reform' is not the same as 'we will advise you on how to reform'
terms.connects: Rules out option B which claims the Bank said it will advise the Sri Lankan government

terms.meaning: The Bank is considering using existing loans to alleviate shortages — it is not offering additional new funds
terms.example: Redirecting an existing loan is different from providing new/additional funds
terms.connects: Rules out option D which says the Bank will provide additional funds

given.plan: Carefully match each option against the exact wording of the passage, watching for overstatements, misattributions, and conclusions that go beyond what was explicitly stated.

steps.title: Evaluate option A
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false when A is true and B is false
steps.apply: Option A says the Bank identified lack of foreign exchange as the ROOT CAUSE. The passage only says the crisis led to a shortage of foreign exchange (an effect), and the Bank called for structural reforms — it never named foreign exchange as the root cause. A is not supported.
steps.note: Cause and effect are not interchangeable in inference questions.

steps.title: Evaluate option B
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false when A is true and B is false
steps.apply: Option B says the Bank stated it will ADVISE the government. The passage only says the Bank said Sri Lanka needed to adopt reforms. Saying a country 'needs to' do something is not the same as saying 'we will advise them on how'. B goes beyond the passage.
steps.note: A prescription ('you need reforms') is different from a commitment to provide guidance.

steps.title: Evaluate option C
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and A, conclude B
steps.apply: The passage says the Bank will not finance until the country has an adequate macroeconomic policy framework. This conditional ('until X happens') directly implies the Bank believes X has not yet happened — i.e., Sri Lanka does not currently have an adequate framework. C follows necessarily.
steps.note: 'Will do Y only when X' implies 'currently not-X' from the speaker's perspective.

steps.title: Evaluate option D
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false when A is true and B is false
steps.apply: Option D says the Bank WILL provide additional funds for essentials. The passage says the Bank is considering using its EXISTING loans to help alleviate shortages — it has explicitly declared it will NOT offer NEW financing. D directly contradicts the passage.
steps.note: Using existing loans is not the same as providing new/additional funds.

solution.result: C. According to the World Bank, Sri Lanka does not yet have an adequate macroeconomic policy framework.

questions.formula_note: Conditional logic ('until X' implies 'currently not-X') is key; the passage-reading skill lies in distinguishing exact wording from overstatements.
